name: Coatlicue
title: Aztec - Coatlicue
symbols: Her most defining feature - a skirt made of intertwined serpents, representing the earth's fertility and dangerous power

primarySources.title: Florentine Codex
primarySources.author: Fray Bernardino de Sahagún
primarySources.text: Sahagún's Florentine Codex provides detailed descriptions of Aztec religious beliefs and practices, including accounts of Coatlicue and her role in the Aztec pantheon. Book 3, in particular, focuses on the origins of the gods.

primarySources.title: Histoyre du Mechique
primarySources.author: André Thévet
primarySources.text: Thévet's Histoyre du Mechique offers insights into Aztec history and religion, including mentions of Coatlicue and her significance as a mother goddess. While not as comprehensive as Sahagún, it provides valuable corroborating information.

primarySources.title: Anales de Cuauhtitlán
primarySources.author: Anonymous
primarySources.text: The Anales de Cuauhtitlán, part of the Codex Chimalpopoca, contains a creation myth that features Coatlicue and the birth of Huitzilopochtli. It offers a different perspective on the events surrounding Coatlicue's pregnancy and the subsequent conflict with her other children.

symbols: Her most defining feature - a skirt made of intertwined serpents, representing the earth's fertility and dangerous power

description:
Coatlicue, whose name translates to "Serpent Skirt" in Nahuatl, is a prominent figure in Aztec mythology, embodying the complex and often paradoxical nature of the earth and motherhood. She is revered as the mother of gods, including the war god Huitzilopochtli, the moon goddess Coyolxauhqui, and the Centzon Huitznahua, the four hundred southern stars. Her image and stories are deeply intertwined with themes of creation, destruction, fertility, death, and sacrifice, reflecting the cyclical worldview of the Aztec people. She is also known by the epithets Teteoinan ("Mother of Gods"), Toci ("Our Grandmother"), Cihuacoatl ("Serpent Woman"), and Tonantzin ("Our Revered Mother").

Coatlicue's origin story, as recounted in various sources including the Florentine Codex compiled by Fray Bernardino de Sahagún, centers around her miraculous pregnancy. While sweeping the temple of Coatepec (Serpent Mountain), a ball of feathers (specifically, hummingbird feathers, according to some accounts) fell upon her, impregnating her. This unexpected event angered her existing children, Coyolxauhqui and the Centzon Huitznahua, who considered it a dishonor to their mother. They plotted to kill her, viewing the pregnancy as a sign of impurity or infidelity. Their anger stemmed from the disruption of the established order and the perceived threat to their own power and status within the cosmic hierarchy.

The most famous myth associated with Coatlicue is the birth of Huitzilopochtli. As Coyolxauhqui and the Centzon Huitznahua approached Coatepec to carry out their plan, Huitzilopochtli sprang forth fully armed from his mother's womb. He immediately defended Coatlicue, slaying Coyolxauhqui and scattering the Centzon Huitznahua across the sky, transforming them into the stars. This myth is not merely a story of birth; it is a powerful allegory of the constant struggle between opposing forces: light and darkness, order and chaos, and the triumph of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon and stars. The dismemberment of Coyolxauhqui is often interpreted as a representation of the moon's phases, while the scattering of the Centzon Huitznahua symbolizes the dispersal of stars at dawn.

The symbolism associated with Coatlicue is rich and multifaceted. Her most striking feature, the skirt of serpents, represents the earth's fertile yet dangerous power. Serpents, in Mesoamerican cultures, were often associated with fertility, the earth, and the underworld. The fact that they form her skirt suggests the all-encompassing nature of her power, encompassing both life and death. Her necklace, made of human hearts and hands, and her skull-like face, further emphasize the connection between life, death, and sacrifice. These elements highlight the Aztec understanding of the world as a place where life is sustained by death and sacrifice is necessary to maintain cosmic balance. The clawed feet suggest her connection to the earth and her power over the terrestrial realm.

Furthermore, Coatlicue is often depicted with dual serpent heads emerging from her neck, where her head should be. This imagery is interpreted in several ways. Some scholars believe it represents the duality of her nature, encompassing both creation and destruction. Others see it as a symbol of the blood that flowed from her neck after Huitzilopochtli was born, representing the life-giving force of sacrifice. The headless depiction can also be linked to the myth of Coyolxauhqui's dismemberment, suggesting a connection between the two figures and the cyclical nature of life and death.

Coatlicue's cultural significance within Aztec society was profound. As the mother of Huitzilopochtli, the patron deity of the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan, she held a central position in the Aztec pantheon. She was venerated in various rituals and ceremonies, often involving offerings and sacrifices. Her image served as a constant reminder of the importance of sacrifice in maintaining cosmic order and ensuring the continued prosperity of the Aztec people. The temple of Coatepec, where the myth of her miraculous pregnancy took place, was a significant pilgrimage site, further highlighting her importance in Aztec religious life. The reenactment of the birth of Huitzilopochtli, where a warrior would symbolically defeat his enemies, mirrored the myth and reinforced the Aztec belief in the power of their patron deity and the necessity of warfare to sustain their empire.

Coatlicue's role as a primordial mother goddess is also significant. She represents the ultimate source of life and creation, but also the inevitability of death and decay. This duality is a key characteristic of many earth mother goddesses across different cultures. She embodies the cyclical nature of existence, where life emerges from death and death gives way to new life. This concept was central to the Aztec worldview, which emphasized the interconnectedness of all things and the importance of maintaining balance within the cosmos. Her association with serpents further reinforces this connection to the earth and the underworld, realms associated with both fertility and death.

While the primary sources provide valuable insights into Coatlicue's mythology and significance, interpretations vary among scholars. Some emphasize her role as a fearsome and powerful deity, highlighting her association with death and sacrifice. Others focus on her nurturing and life-giving aspects, emphasizing her role as a mother goddess. However, it is generally agreed that Coatlicue represents a complex and multifaceted figure, embodying the fundamental principles of the Aztec worldview. Her enduring presence in Aztec art and mythology serves as a testament to her lasting impact on the culture and beliefs of the Aztec people.

In conclusion, Coatlicue is more than just a mother goddess; she is a symbol of the earth's power, the cyclical nature of life and death, and the importance of sacrifice in maintaining cosmic balance. Her myths and imagery offer a profound glimpse into the complex religious beliefs and worldview of the Aztec civilization. The study of Coatlicue provides valuable insights into the broader themes of creation, destruction, and renewal that are central to human understanding of the world around us.
